tweets.text: Well there's a reason why we said this isn't an "AI bearish index" or for that matter "AI bullish index". We merely said that it's not necessarily bearish, esp in the long run, that token index continues to travel down, which is also the point @Conor_Coops made. If we transition from a state, in which two closed models dominate and are the the source of most compute demand, towards a state, in which many enterprises rent/own compute and host own post-trained open models, the total compute market could indeed become bigger if not substantially bigger. That is a question of demand elasticity and ultimate ROI from wider AI adoption into workflows. I think it's possible to hold two thoughts in one's brain at the same time?

Agree with last paragraph that they're playing a different game.

Slightly disagree with second paragraph because if you look to every other industry China has done this to (eg EVs), the playbook is clear. There is no tasteful exploit - it's full boar guns blazing and in many regards it has worked given their ability to become a leader or very close second in most of the spaces they've attempted this strategy.

tweets.text:
TVL is the laziest way to evaluate a protocol.

That becomes clear when you compare @LidoFinance and @jito_sol.

Jito ($786M) vs Lido ($17.7B).
Lido is 22x larger by TVL.

But their annual fees?

→ Lido: $740M
→ Jito: $259M

Lido mostly earns fees from staking rewards on deposited ETH.

Jito earns fees from staking + MEV + priority fees through its block engine on Solana.

Most of Jito's fees come from MEV and priority fees across Solana's blockspace, so it earns more per dollar deposited.

Capital size ≠ capital efficiency.

tweets.text:
Grabbed some $EWY at 155.52, $AAOI at $93, $NBIS at 166.3, $SNDK at 1395. AAOI will be a longer term position for me, same with NBIS. Ewy and SNDK ill dump within the week most likely. 

The one thing I would caution however is there's a very real possibility that we have some extended chop/sideways period due to the amount of deleveraging that occurred. I think it would be quite easy to get harmed each direction. At least until vol dies off a bit. These entries were the result of waiting 6 weeks. The market structure is pretty poor now overall, so I'd rather keep the higher conviction stuff, and start building those positions. As we could easily bleed lower. Will go into more of my detailed thinking tomorrow when I have some time. But the 6 weeks of waiting for these looks to be off to a great start each direction

tweets.text:
Just dropped a practical breakdown of founder growth on X.

Here’s your TL;DR ↓

- In B2B, buying decisions are shaped by hidden buyers, internal champions, and execs – not just the visible contact.

- For crypto teams, X still matters. X, Telegram, and YouTube make up 84% of crypto community social time.

- In fact, founder growth is a trust system, not a posting-frequency problem.

- The common failure mode is volume without position – daily posts, recycled threads, and AI-polished founder copy.

- At @GREEND0TS we usually map the fix into seven layers: expertise zones, expertise circles, tone of voice, long-form authority, QTs/comments, relationship density, and dynamic optimization.

- AI is useful for research, clustering, and first drafts. It should not replace the founder’s judgment, editing, or point of view.

- The real signal is relationship density: replies, DMs, intros, and inbound from the right circles matter more than raw follower count.

tweets.text:
Your decision should be to stop hitting the ATM at $100 (only ATM at your own discretion anywhere above $100) and to stop trying to target a $99-$100 range because it's literally impossible. Best range you can get is probably $95-$105

The STRC trilemma is that you can't have these 3 things at the same time:

- high market cap for STRC (ie scale)
- dividend rate that only adjusts once a month and by 50bps max (ie quasi stable on short timeframes)
- a tight 1% price peg

tweets.text:
2026 is the year enterprises require multi-model support in every AI purchasing decision.

Betting on one provider is too risky when the frontier keeps changing hands. Sometimes OpenAI is ahead, sometimes Anthropic, and for many smaller tasks an open-source model is already good enough. Enterprises do not need Einstein-level intelligence to classify an email, summarize a document, or update a spreadsheet.

At the frontier, a model can command a 90% premium. But once it is no longer at the frontier, it loses its pricing power and has to compete on cost, pushing inference toward thinner margins.

That is why the defining enterprise AI infrastructure of 2026 will be the harness: model-agnostic systems that evaluate each task and route it to the best model for intelligence, speed, security, and cost. Multi-model will become a baseline procurement requirement.

Also if intelligence really becomes a commodity that is too cheap to meter, it could generate enormous revenue while still looking economically more like a utility: massive consumption, massive infrastructure, but thin margins.

The challenge is that today’s high inference margins at the frontier help fund the enormous compute required to train the next generation of models. Compress those margins too quickly, and labs have less capital to buy compute. Less compute could mean slower progress at the frontier, creating a vicious cycle.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: Kimi K3 may be an important inflection point for AI. Potentially negative for Anthropic and OpenAI while being net positive for essentially every other company in the world. I mean that very literally. Although the real “Sputnik moment” would be an open-source frontier model that https://t.co/tdAkU4oRgo
tweets.full_text:
2026 is the year enterprises require multi-model support in every AI purchasing decision.

Betting on one provider is too risky when the frontier keeps changing hands. Sometimes OpenAI is ahead, sometimes Anthropic, and for many smaller tasks an open-source model is already good enough. Enterprises do not need Einstein-level intelligence to classify an email, summarize a document, or update a spreadsheet.

At the frontier, a model can command a 90% premium. But once it is no longer at the frontier, it loses its pricing power and has to compete on cost, pushing inference toward thinner margins.

That is why the defining enterprise AI infrastructure of 2026 will be the harness: model-agnostic systems that evaluate each task and route it to the best model for intelligence, speed, security, and cost. Multi-model will become a baseline procurement requirement.

Also if intelligence really becomes a commodity that is too cheap to meter, it could generate enormous revenue while still looking economically more like a utility: massive consumption, massive infrastructure, but thin margins.

The challenge is that today’s high inference margins at the frontier help fund the enormous compute required to train the next generation of models. Compress those margins too quickly, and labs have less capital to buy compute. Less compute could mean slower progress at the frontier, creating a vicious cycle.

tweets.text:
This is where the rubber hits the road in Coastal Metropolitan areas.  

The infrastructure was built 100 yrs ago and most of these cities are so expensive now, fixing the infrastructure doesn't actually make fiscal sense when you add in the cost vs. all the other budgeted items.

This is part of the generational arbitrage.

Texas has a massive, record-setting "rainy day" fund balance of over $24 billion, in addition to a recent multi-billion dollar budget surplus used for historic property tax relief. In contrast, California faced persistent structural deficits that required a revised $351.7 billion state budget to achieve a balanced status with zero deficit through mid-2028.

Living in NYC, Chicago, LA or SF is like a tightening vice on anyone that owns property there.

It's a slow motion generational trainwreck especially for public employees like cops, firefighters & teachers!!
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
The 100 year old pipe that just sinkholed Sunset Boulevard was already on LA's replacement list. Construction was scheduled for 2031.

LADWP budgeted $280 million to replace 6.4 miles of this exact 36-inch trunk line. The project is still in the design phase. Work starts in 2031

tweets.text:
My current thesis:

“corpo” chains that pursue security-first neutrality will do exceptional well over the medium to long term. 

The Overton window on intervention is rapidly changing. 

People sick & tired of hackers winning. 

Solutions that can prevent and recover large scale defi hacks in a neutral manner will be increasingly popular among users & projects.

Impossible to do on Ethereum which will serve a difference audience & market:  maximum need for censorship resistance.

thread_tweets.text:
If these type of features are implemented, there will be for sure a fight between objective and publicly verifiable events (that are desirable to help with -- like hacks) and subjective / probabilistic confidence events (i.e., is this address really a bad actor?). 

I think that is a fight worth having though; since objective + publicly verifiable is generally good; subjectivity is bad.

I think that is a fight worth having though; since objective + publicly verifiable is generally good; subjectivity is bad.
